title: Model Selection: The Gates, Not the Leaderboard
subtitle: Five hard constraints eliminate candidates before quality ever gets a vote.
source_attribution: Derived from this chapter's own reviewed prose; see the chapter's source_attribution for the underlying upstream reference. See CREDITS.md.

methodCards.name: Model A — Flagship
bullets: Clears every gate: latency, compliance, context, tool-use
Loses to Model B on cost alone

methodCards.name: Model B — Smaller sibling
bullets: Clears every gate, including the tool-use test

whenToChoose.heading: Route by task complexity when
bullets: Task mix varies widely across your traffic
Your complexity classifier beats a coin flip

whenToChoose.heading: Self-host to clear compliance when
bullets: Data residency rules out every API provider
Volume justifies 0.5-1 FTE of on-call engineering

whenToChoose.heading: Stick with one flagship model when
bullets: Volume is low enough that the cost gap is a rounding error
Routing logic would cost more to build than it saves

workedExample.heading: Customer-support agent: which model?
workedExample.body: 200,000 tickets/month, PII can't leave EU infrastructure, p95 under 3 seconds, budget capped at $6,000/month. Gate 1 (latency) rules out extended-thinking tiers. Gate 2 (compliance) cuts the pool roughly in half. Gate 3 (context) eliminates nothing — the ~3,000-token working set is trivial for everyone left. Gate 4 (tool-use) eliminates the cheapest remaining candidate: it got the refund schema wrong 12% of the time on a 100-ticket test. Two survivors reach Gate 5 (cost): Model A at $4,500/month, Model B at $900/month. Both cleared every gate — Model B wins by 5x.

stat.label: Model B's cost as a share of the $6,000 budget

coreIntuition.heading: Constraints eliminate before quality ever votes
coreIntuition.body: Latency, compliance, context length, and tool-use quality each eliminate candidates before cost or benchmark score gets a say. By the time you're comparing quality, you're only comparing whatever survived the gates that actually decide whether the system works in production.

interviewQuestions.question: The client insists on the flagship model regardless of cost — how do you push back?
interviewQuestions.hint: Show the gates it never needed to clear, propose routing.

interviewQuestions.question: Two models tie on your evaluation set — how do you break the tie?
interviewQuestions.hint: Cost at real volume, then rate limits at P99, SDK maturity, provider reliability.

interviewQuestions.question: A regulatory change suddenly requires in-region processing — what breaks?
interviewQuestions.hint: Whatever passed gate 2 before might not now.

productionConsiderations.text: Test tool-use quality on your own schema, not a generic benchmark — accuracy varies by how your tools are named and typed.
Test effective context recall at your real document length — advertised max-context numbers overstate what's usable.
Treat compliance as gate 2, not an afterthought — if a candidate can't legally touch the data, it was never a candidate.
Re-run all five gates when volume, task mix, or pricing changes — a decision correct at launch can go wrong by next quarter.

checklist.text: Latency budget defined — interactive vs. batch, a real p95 target
Compliance and data-residency requirements checked before quality
Effective context tested at real document length, not the advertised max
Tool-use accuracy tested on your own schema and tools
Cost priced at real volume and token mix, not the sticker rate

keyTakeaways: Model selection is a sequence of gates, not a leaderboard lookup — apply them in order
Whatever survives all five gates is your candidate pool; pick the cheapest one that clears your quality bar
Effective context and real tool-use accuracy routinely differ from what's advertised — test both yourself
Re-run the gates periodically — prices, context windows, and tool-use quality all move quarter to quarter

nodes.label: Compliance
nodes.sublabel: Can the provider touch this data?
